coredata.dc:title: Self-assembling reduced graphene quantum dots on hematite photoanode for passivating surface states toward significantly improved water splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2016 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Jiale
Chen, Jing
Li, Chang Ming
coredata.dc:description: Hematite is a promising photoanode material but limited by its large overpotential (0.5–0.6 V) toward water splitting. The slow kinetics and high density of trapping states are the main factors. The former could be improved by catalyst modification for improved kinetics, while the latter is normally suppressed by passivating trapping states with atomic layer deposition-formed conformal overlayers. Up to date only a few of oxides and carbon materials were used to passivate hematite photoanode via wet chemical method, such as Al2O3, TiO2 and reduced graphene oxide sheets. Herein, rGQDs as another carbon material are self-assembly onto hematite for the first time, which remarkably increases the photocurrent by 8.0 times at 1.23 V. The passivation of surface states confirmed by photoluminescence spectroscopy has significantly change in the radiative recombination, and can used well explain the great improvement. Importantly, this work clearly demonstrates a mass-productive and inexpensive solution-based self-assembly approach is also an efficient surface passivation technique.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water splitting
Hematite
Reduced graphene quantum dot
Passivation
Self-assembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and crystal structure of Fe2O3 nanostructure electrodes. (a and b) Top-view SEM images. (c) Section-view SEM image. (d) XRD pattern of Fe2O3 on FTO conductive glass.

TEM images of bare Fe2O3 and rGQDs modified Fe2O3. (a and b) Low-magnification TEM image of bare Fe2O3. (c) Lattice resolution TEM image of bare Fe2O3. (d) Low-magnification TEM image of rGQDs modified Fe2O3. (e) Lattice resolution TEM image of rGQDs modified Fe2O3 on single crystalline Fe2O3 zone. (f) Lattice resolution TEM image of bare Fe2O3 nanostructure edge. (g) Lattice resolution TEM image of rGQDs modified Fe2O3 nanostructure edge.

Optical properties of fabricated electrodes and synthesized GOQDs. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of bare Fe2O3 film and rGQDs modified. Inset shows the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of GOQDs in aqueous solution. (b) PL emission spectra of GOQDs aqueous solution. (c) FTIR spectra of fabricated Fe2O3 electrode and rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrode.

Photoelectrochemical and AC impedance measurements. (a) Linear-sweep voltammogram (LSV) curves of rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes with various amounts of rGQDs and bare Fe2O3 electrodes. (b) Transient photocurrent response plots at 1.6 V (vs. RHE). (c) Nyquist plots at 1.0 V (vs. RHE) under illumination. Inset is the enlargement of part Nyquist plots. (d) Mott–Schottky plots at frequency of 1000 Hz in the dark.
PL emission spectra of Fe2O3 and rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes.

Schematic mechanism explanation for performance enhancement. (a) Energy level structure and trapping states of bare Fe2O3 electrode. (b) Energy level structure and passivated trapping states of rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrode. The excitation, transportation and recombination of charge are also shown in this schematic.

head.ce:title: Self-assembling reduced graphene quantum dots on hematite photoanode for passivating surface states toward significantly improved water splitting

Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Faculty of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hematite is a promising photoanode material but limited by its large overpotential (0.5–0.6 V) toward water splitting. The slow kinetics and high density of trapping states are the main factors. The former could be improved by catalyst modification for improved kinetics, while the latter is normally suppressed by passivating trapping states with atomic layer deposition-formed conformal overlayers. Up to date only a few of oxides and carbon materials were used to passivate hematite photoanode via wet chemical method, such as Al2O3, TiO2 and reduced graphene oxide sheets. Herein, rGQDs as another carbon material are self-assembly onto hematite for the first time, which remarkably increases the photocurrent by 8.0 times at 1.23 V. The passivation of surface states confirmed by photoluminescence spectroscopy has significantly change in the radiative recombination, and can used well explain the great improvement. Importantly, this work clearly demonstrates a mass-productive and inexpensive solution-based self-assembly approach is also an efficient surface passivation technique.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting to produce hydrogen as a clean and renewable energy can directly convert solar energy into chemical energy in the form of molecular bonds, analogous to the photosynthesis process exploited by nature [1–3]. Hematite (α-Fe2O3) is an attractive material as a photoanode in PEC water splitting due to its ability to absorb light across much of the visible spectrum (band gap ∼2.1 eV), high PEC stability in alkaline solution, and earth abundance [4,5]. The main drawbacks of hematite include slow kinetics, low mobility of holes, a short hole lifetime and a high density of surface states for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at its surface [4]. The slow kinetics can be overcome with some improved catalysts such as cobalt-phosphate (Co-Pi), MoSx and Co(OH)2/Co3O4 [6–9]. The low mobility of holes and the short hole lifetime cause high charge recombination in the bulk. Generally, doping and nanostructuring are two main strategies to remedy the short board, which include doping with Pt, Ti, Sn, Mo, Cr and Si, as well as some unique nanostructures such as dendritic, nanowire, nanoporous array, and nanotube array structures [10–19].

Benjamin Klahr et al. have demonstrated the existence of a surface state at the semiconductor/liquid interface where holes accumulate inducing the shift of the measured flat-band potential and the large overpotential [20]. An Al2O3 overlayer synthesized with atomic layer deposition (ALD) was used to passivate the hematite photoanode can increase the photocurrent by a factor of 3.5 at +1.0 V (vs. RHE) [21]. Ultrathin ALD TiO2 overlayer also shows enhanced photocurrent after high temperature annealing (>650 °C) [22]. It is known that ALD technique requires high vacuum condition and expensive equipment but the productivity is low. Therefore, a wet chemistry approach is highly expected to prepare the passivating conformal overlayers.

Hisatomi et al. have used a chemical bath deposition to synthesize Al2O3, Ga2O3 and In2O3 overlayers on thin hematite films (27–30 nm), leading to a cathodic shift in onset potential of 70 mV, 250 mV and 150 mV, respectively, in comparison to the bare hematite [23]. However, the photocurrent density at +1.23 V (vs. RHE) doesn't show increasing, which may be due to the low conductivity of these oxides. Recently, TiO2 overlayer was synthesized from aqueous solution of titanium bis(ammonium lactate) dihydroxide (TALH) by TALH self-assembly [24], and the fabricated Fe2O3/TiO2 electrode exhibits a photocurrent ∼4.5 times higher than a bare hematite electrode. Therefore self-assembly could be a better wet chemistry method to functionalize the surface of electrodes efficiently with the conformal overlayer. Yang Hou et al. have reported that the graphene sheets as an interlayer can ‘wrap’ the Fe2O3 nanorod arrays for fast charge transfer [25]. After graphene sheets modification, small improvement of photoconversion efficiency is achieved from 0.06% to 0.08%. Since the improvement is due to the partial graphene coating on Fe2O3 nanorod arrays, the Fe2O3 electrode has no an eventually entire change, and the similar cases are observed in Fe2O3 nanostructures modified with rGO sheets [26,27]. In our previous work, GQDs modified ZnO nanowire arrays significantly enhanced the photocurrent density due to the better visible light absorption of GQDs [28], and the GQDs can well wrap the nanowire arrays. Therefore we could expect to self-assemble good conformal overlayers for perfectly passivating the surface states of Fe2O3.

Herein, Fe2O3 nanostructure electrodes were passivated with reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) through self-assembly technique followed by post heat treatment. The photocurrent density is greatly improved by a factor of 8.0 at +1.23 V (vs. RHE) under standard illumination conditions. The passivation of surface states is revealed and confirmed by photoluminescence spectroscopy. Importantly, this work clearly demonstrates that the mass-productive solution self-assembly approach is an efficient surface passivation technique in comparison to the complicated and expensive ALD technique.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: GOQDs were synthesized from Vulcan XC-72 carbon black by refluxed with concentrated nitric acid [28]. 0.25 g XC-72 carbon black was put into 50 mL 6 mol L−1 HNO3, which was refluxed for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the suspension was centrifuged for 30 min to obtain a yellow supernatant. The supernatant was heated to remove water and nitric acid, and GOQDs a reddish-brown solid were obtained. Before the use, GOQDs solid was dissolved in aqueous solution and sonicated for 10 min.

The hematite photoanodes were prepared via a simple solution-based method and two-step annealing [29]. First, a starting material is grown on substrate (FTO) at 100 °C in aqueous solution containing Fe precursor (0.15 M FeCl3·6H2O). After the growth process, the substrates were washed in distilled water and allowed to dry in air at room temperature. The mixed phases, such as akaganeite, goethite and hematite, were the first phases obtained, then the initial annealing at 550 °C for 1 h followed by 15 min-short annealing at 700 °C. In a typical procedure, the Fe2O3 was immersed in 5 mL GOQDs aqueous solution (pH = 7) for 5 h. After slow evaporation at 60 °C in vacuum oven, the GOQDs/Fe2O3 was produced. Then, the rGQDs/Fe2O3 was prepared through annealing treatment on GOQDs/Fe2O3 at 450 °C in argon stream for 1 h. Finally, the sample was washed with water before further characterization.

The morphology characterizations were conducted with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F, JEOL) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL). The crystal structure analyses were performed by using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, XRD-7000, Shimadzu). The fluorescence and surface functional groups were determined by fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet-6700, Thermo Scientific). Photoluminescence spectra of electrodes were measured at room temperature using a 520 nm excitation light in air. The optical properties of fabricated electrodes were characterized with a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–Vis spectrophotometer in the spectral range of 300–900 nm.

The photoelectrochemical and AC impedance measurements were carried out in a standard 3-electrode cell using the hematite, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and Pt plate as the counter electrode. The electrolyte is 1.0 M NaOH aqueous solution. The experiments were conducted using a 150 W Xe lamp as the light source and the illumination intensity near the electrode surface was 100 mW cm−2. Photoresponses were recorded with an electrochemical analyzer (660D, CHI Instruments). Nyquist plots were measured at 1.0 V (vs. RHE) with an alternating current signal (10 mV) under illumination in the frequency range of 2–105 Hz. Mott–Schottky plots were recorded at 1000 Hz in the dark.

The morphology and XRD pattern of the fabricated Fe2O3 nanostructure electrodes in Fig. 1 show that the top surface of Fe2O3 film has worm-like structure with meso- and macro-pores, which is converted from β-FeOOH nanostructure by two-step annealing. This film is composed of ∼72 nm necked particles. The cross-section indicates the thickness of this film is around 404.3 nm. The XRD analysis of the film as in Fig. 1d displays a good single crystalline character and the peak pattern is well consistent with the standard pattern of α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS 01-085-0599). The (110) lattice plane peak is dominant possibly due to preferential orientation of the α-Fe2O3 nanostructure.

Since optical absorption properties are important to understand electrode performance for solar energy conversion, they are investigated with UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–vis spectra of bare and rGQDs modified Fe2O3 electrodes. The rGQDs overlayer can slightly enhance the light absorption below 550 nm. Actually the rGQDs coating doesn't change the band gap of Fe2O3 electrode due to an almost same absorption edge. The band gap of Fe2O3 obtained from Fig. 3a is 2.06 eV, which is consistent with the value of Fe2O3 synthesized from the reported work [29]. The inset of Fig. 3a indicates that the GOQDs have a broad absorption below 600 nm. In Fig. 3b, the emission wavelengths of synthesized GOQDs are excitation-dependent, exhibiting a red shift with increasing excitation wavelength, which might be caused by the different emissive sites of GOQDs [31]. This indirectly demonstrates the successful synthesis of GOQDs and is agreement with other reported carbonaceous quantum dots [28,32]. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) characterizations confirm that rGQDs are successfully coated onto the Fe2O3 electrodes. In Fig. 3c, the characteristic peaks at 465 cm−1 and 545 cm−1 can be identified from both samples, which are attributed to vibrations of the Fe–O functional groups [33]. rGQDs modification introduces many oxygen-containing groups, including carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups, which anchoring to the edges and onto the basal plane of rGQDs [34]. One peak at 2571 cm−1 from S-H group also is detected, which may be due to S element in the carbon black.

The photoelectrochemical measurements were conducted in a 3-electrode cell with an electrolyte of 1.0 M NaOH. In Fig. 4 a, in dark the LSV curves of bare Fe2O3 and rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes are almost same, indicating no catalytic effect from rGQDs modification. Under illumination, the photocurrent density is improved as the amount of rGQDs increasing but decreased when the concentration of GOQDs is higher than 0.05 mg ml−1. Therefore the optimal concentration of GOQDs is 0.05 mg ml−1. The real amount of rGQDs is also obtained by quantitative analysis using energy-dispersive spectrometry as shown in Table S1. The observed amount-dependant behaviour suggests the amount of GOQDs can affect the passivation level or/and the charge transfer at semiconductor/electrolyte interface (SEI). Then this can affect the efficient charge separation and the final photocurrent density. At potential of 1.23 V (vs. RHE), the photocurrent density of the rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrode increases by a factor of 8.0 (from 0.11 mA cm−2 to 0.88 mA cm−2) versus the bare Fe2O3. This enhancement factor is significantly higher than that passivated with ALD Al2O3 layers (enhancement factor: 3.5) and TiO2 layers via self-assembly (enhancement factor: 4.5) [21,24]. The photocurrent density can come up to 1.8 mA cm−2 at +1.5 V (vs RHE), which is superior to the rGO sheets modified [26,27]. Moreover, the transient photoresponse and stability were determined by amperometry as shown in Fig. 4b. All the electrodes show a good sensitivity at this potential and good stability during measuring time. The long term stability tests are shown in Fig. S1. Elimination the influence of electrolyte evaporation, both bare Fe2O3 and rGQDs/Fe2O3 exhibit good stability but the later has much higher current density. When light is turned on, a sharp anodic current spike appears due to charge recombination in the bulk or/and at the SEI. Conversely, when the light is turned off the cathodic transient peak observed and can be ascribed to electrons diffusing from the external circuit and recombining with the accumulated holes at the SEI [21]. The cathodic transient peaks of these electrodes are replotted and shown in Fig. S2. The relative height of theses peaks decrease as the amount of rGQDs increasing to a critical value (GOQDs concentration: 0.05 mg ml−1), decreasing while beyond this value. This clearly indicates the certain amount of rGQDs can efficiently reduce the holes accumulation at the SEI.

To reveal the insights at SEI, AC impedance measurements are performed and shown in Fig. 4c and d. In Fig. 4c, Nyquist plot of rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes shows smaller semicircle at the high frequency range than that of bare Fe2O3 electrodes, revealing better charge transfer at the SEI, which is similar to graphene-based electrode [35,36]. Furthermore, the equivalent series resistance (ESR) of rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes obtained from the intercept at the real part axis is smaller than that of control (as shown in the inset of Fig. 4c), suggesting much lower resistance of rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrode than that of the bare Fe2O3 electrode. Mott–Schottky plots further confirm that rGQDs coating greatly decreases the slope of Mott–Schottky plot, indicating the enhanced electron density. This is greatly different from the reported metal oxides as overlayers, which have lower electron concentration [23,24,37]. In addition, both samples deliver the same flat-band voltage, confirming just surface-state passivation effect is existence, not the catalytic effect.

The passivation of surface trapping states are able to affect the recombination pathways of the photo-excited holes at the SEI. Florian Le Formal et al. has demonstrated that the conformal overlayers of Al2O3 can eliminate the surface trapping states to induce an increased photoluminescence quantum yield [21]. Because surface traps play a critical role in the nonradiative recombination of excitons present at the surface in Fe2O3 [38], and if the surface states are even partially eliminated by rGQDs coating, photoluminescence should be enhanced. Fig. 5 shows the results of a PL spectra study for the bare Fe2O3 and rGQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes without applied bias. Under excitation light of 520 nm, the control photoanode shows a weak and broad emission at around 585 nm. The electrode with rGQDs modification displays enhanced PL intensity at around 579 nm. This enhanced emission shows more photogenerated excitons are recombined radiatively. The enhanced PL intensity after rGQDs self-assembly is a strong evidence that a fraction of the inter-bandgap surface states are passivated by the rGQDs overlayer, similar to Al2O3 overlayer [21].

Various analytical techniques confirm the presence of surface trapping states on the nanostructured hematite photoanodes. Physically, the energetic traps could be a result of a disordered crystalline surface creating dangling bonds or oxygen vacancies [39]. The energy-level structure and trapping states of bare Fe2O3 electrode are schematically shown in Fig. 6 a. Photoexcitation of α-Fe2O3 generates electron-hole pairs in the bulk and depletion layer. Photogenerated electrons migrate to the FTO and pass through the circuit to the Pt counter electrode. Photogenerated holes migrate to the SEI for water splitting. Moreover, the charge recombination will happen in the bulk and at the SEI. Due to the surface defect states in the band structure, high charge recombination and inefficient water splitting by the photogenerated holes strongly suppress the water splitting. With rGQDs passivation layer, the surface trapping states are mainly passivated through eliminating the dangling bonds or oxygen vacancies (Fig. 6b). Further X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations of bare Fe2O3 and rGQDs/Fe2O3 (Fig. S3) clearly show that the O1s peak position shifts to higher binding energy, while the Fe2p and C1s peaks have almost the same positions. The charge recombination can be greatly reduced at the SEI, thus the improved photocurrent density can be observed.

In summary, nanostructured Fe2O3 electrodes were passivated with rGQDs by self-assembly technique followed by post heat treatment. The photocurrent density is improved by a factor of 8.0 at +1.23 V vs. RHE under standard illumination conditions than the bare Fe2O3 electrodes. Further investigation by analyzing transient photocurrents, Nyquist plots, Mott–Schottky plots and photoluminescence spectra provided convincing evidence that the enhanced photocurrent density is due to the passivation of surface trapping states. Importantly, this work clearly demonstrates that a mass-productive and inexpensive solution-based self-assembly approach is also an efficient surface passivation technique toward PEC water splitting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant NO. XDJK2016C005), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant NO. 2015M582495), Start-up grant from Southwest University (Grant NO. SWU111071) and Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (Grant NO. cstc2012gjhz90002).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2016.03.066.
